title: Fix Our Forests Act
stage_label: Committee on Agriculture, Nutrition, and Forestry Subcommittee on Conservation, Forestry, Natural Resources, and Biotech
plain_summary: The Fix Our Forests Act creates new rules for managing forests on government land to reduce wildfire risks. The law sets up special high-risk wildfire areas and creates a government center that different agencies work together to track and predict fires. It makes it faster and easier for the government to approve forest management projects by reducing paperwork and limiting how long people can challenge these projects in court. The law also supports programs to help communities prepare for wildfires, restore damaged forests, and help wildland firefighters and their families.
last_action_text: Committee on Agriculture, Nutrition, and Forestry Subcommittee on Conservation, Forestry, Natural Resources, and Biotechnology. Hearings held. With printed Hearing: S.Hrg. 119-27.
subjects: Wilderness and natural areas, wildlife refuges, wild rivers, habitats

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Fix Our Forests Act. The law includes several parts that are organized in a table of contents showing what topics each section covers.

sections.title: Section 2  Definitions
sections.text: This section explains what important terms mean throughout the law. The Director means the person in charge of the Fireshed Center. A fireshed is a large area of land that faces similar wildfire dangers and where actions could help control wildfire results. A fireshed management project is a specific project done under section one hundred six. The Fireshed Registry is a database set up under section one hundred three. A forest plan means different types of land use plans made by government agencies for managing forests and public lands. A Governor means the top elected official of a state, Indian tribe, or Puerto Rico. Hazardous fuels management activities are any work done to manage plants and trees to reduce wildfire risk, like cutting trees, controlled burning, timber harvesting, and grazing. Several terms like at-risk community and wildland-urban interface are defined the same way as in the Healthy Forests Restoration Act of two thousand three. Indian forest land or rangeland means land held in trust by the United States for Indian tribes that has forests or grass cover or could be restored to have such cover. An Indian Tribe has the same meaning as in the Indian Self-Determination and Education Assistance Act. National Forest System lands means lands managed by the Forest Service. Public lands means lands managed by the Bureau of Land Management, including certain railroad grant lands. Relevant Congressional Committees means specific House and Senate committees that oversee natural resources and agriculture. A responsible official is a government employee who can make decisions about proposed actions. The Secretaries means both the Secretary of the Interior and the Secretary of Agriculture. The Secretary means the Secretary of Agriculture. The Secretary concerned means the Secretary of Agriculture for Forest Service lands and the Secretary of the Interior for other public lands. A special district is a local government unit with budget control created for specific purposes. A State includes all fifty states, Washington DC, and US territories.

sections.title: Section 101  Designation of Fireshed Management Areas
sections.text: For the first five years after this law is passed, certain areas will be designated as fireshed management areas. These areas must be large landscape areas that were identified as high wildfire risk in the Forest Service Wildfire Crisis Strategy from January two thousand twenty two. They must also be in the top twenty percent of the seven thousand six hundred eighty eight firesheds published by the Rocky Mountain Research Station in two thousand nineteen for wildfire exposure based on specific criteria. The criteria include wildfire exposure and risk to communities including risk to life and buildings, wildfire exposure and risk to water supplies including tribal water systems, and risk of forests being permanently changed by wildfire. These areas cannot overlap with each other. They can contain both government and private land including Indian forest lands and rangelands. Government agencies must carry out fireshed management projects in these areas. Every five years after the first five years, the Secretary of Agriculture working with the Secretary of the Interior must submit an updated map of firesheds to Congress based on the Fireshed Registry. Within sixty days of submitting the map, they must designate additional fireshed management areas that are in the top twenty percent of firesheds at wildfire risk based on the same criteria. Designating these fireshed management areas does not require following the National Environmental Policy Act.

sections.title: Section 102  Fireshed Center
sections.text: The Secretary of Agriculture working through the Chief of the Forest Service and the Secretary of the Interior working through the Director of the US Geological Survey must work together to create an interagency center called the Fireshed Center. The Center will have four divisions: Technology and Engineering, Data Services, Analysis and Prediction, and Education and Consultation. The Center must include at least one career representative from fifteen different government agencies including the Forest Service, Bureau of Land Management, National Park Service, Bureau of Indian Affairs, US Fish and Wildlife Service, US Geological Survey, Department of Defense, Department of Homeland Security, Department of Energy, Federal Emergency Management Agency, National Science Foundation, National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration, National Aeronautics and Space Administration, National Institute of Standards and Technology, and United States Fire Administration. Each agency head will appoint their representative. These representatives will vote to choose a Director of the Center who must be an employee of either the US Geological Survey or Forest Service. The Director serves for up to seven years and can serve one additional seven-year term. The Director manages and operates the Center and can appoint Associate Directors as needed. The Secretaries can appoint additional representatives from other agencies, states, tribes, or local governments. The Center has eight main purposes: to assess and predict fire and smoke across different areas to help with land management, community risk reduction, public health protection, fire response, and post-fire recovery activities; to provide data collection and science-based decision support services; to reduce duplication across government agencies; to promote coordination and data sharing about wildland fire and smoke decisions; to streamline buying processes for technologies and security systems; to distribute existing data, models, technologies, and forecasts while avoiding duplicate efforts; to maintain the Fireshed Registry; and to share data tools and decision support products with government agencies, tribes, state and local governments, academic institutions, firefighting organizations, and other appropriate entities. The Center can enter into agreements with state governments, tribes, local governments, academic institutions, and private entities to improve its information and operations. The Secretary of the Interior and Secretary of Agriculture must make their personnel available to support the Center and can share management and operational costs. Nothing in this section affects the ownership of data sources.
